name: Biology Advanced
description: An advanced high school biology course providing in-depth exploration of cell biology, molecular genetics, biotechnology, evolutionary mechanisms, animal physiology, and conservation biology.

modules.title: Cell Biology Review & Extension
modules.titleZh: 细胞生物学复习与拓展
modules.objectives: Review prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell ultrastructure at the electron microscope level; Explain the endomembrane system and vesicular trafficking; Describe the role of the cytoskeleton in cell shape and movement; Analyze experimental evidence for the endosymbiotic theory.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Cellular Organelles and Their Functions
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Amoeba Sisters — Cell City Analogy (Extended Organelle Functions)
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Advanced Cell Structure Practice
modules.assignment: Write a 500-word essay arguing for or against the endosymbiotic theory using at least four lines of experimental evidence (genetic, structural, biochemical, and evolutionary), and discuss one piece of counterevidence that must be addressed.

modules.title: Biochemistry Deep Dive
modules.objectives: Explain enzyme kinetics including Km and Vmax; Describe competitive, noncompetitive, and uncompetitive inhibition; Relate protein tertiary and quaternary structure to function; Analyze how allosteric regulation controls metabolic pathways.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Enzyme Structure and Catalysis
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Enzyme Kinetics and Inhibition
modules.assignment: Given a Michaelis-Menten graph showing three curves (uninhibited, competitive inhibitor, and noncompetitive inhibitor), identify each curve, explain the biochemical basis of each inhibition type, and discuss how feedback inhibition of a metabolic pathway relates to allosteric regulation.

modules.title: Genetics & Chromosomal Theory
modules.objectives: Explain how Morgan's fruit fly experiments established the chromosomal theory of inheritance; Calculate recombination frequencies and map distances between linked genes; Describe chromosomal abnormalities including aneuploidy and structural rearrangements; Explain the genetic basis of sex determination.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 13: Modern Understandings of Inheritance
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Chromosomal Theory of Inheritance
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Genetic Linkage and Recombination
modules.assignment: Given testcross data for two linked genes producing 480 offspring (specific phenotypic counts provided), calculate the map distance in centimorgans, draw a partial chromosome map, and explain how crossing over generates recombinant phenotypes.

modules.title: Molecular Biology: DNA Replication & Repair
modules.titleZh: 分子生物学：DNA复制与修复
modules.objectives: Describe DNA replication at the molecular level including the roles of all key enzymes; Explain the differences between leading and lagging strand synthesis; Describe major DNA damage types and their repair mechanisms; Explain how errors in replication or repair can lead to mutation and cancer.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 14: DNA Structure and Function
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — DNA Replication and Repair
modules.video2Note: YouTube — TED-Ed — How DNA Replication Works
modules.assignment: Create an annotated diagram of the replication fork showing all enzymes and their functions; then write a paragraph explaining nucleotide excision repair and why defects in this pathway cause xeroderma pigmentosum.

modules.title: Gene Expression & Proteomics
modules.titleZh: 基因表达与蛋白质组学
modules.objectives: Describe transcription and translation in detail for both prokaryotes and eukaryotes; Explain post-translational modification and protein trafficking; Describe how epigenetic modifications regulate gene expression without altering DNA sequence; Introduce the concept of the proteome and tools used to study it.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 15: Genes and Proteins
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Stated Clearly — From DNA to Protein: Translation
modules.assignment: Compare and contrast transcription and translation between a prokaryote (E. coli) and a eukaryote (human cell), creating a table with at least six key differences; then explain how DNA methylation of a gene's promoter CpG island could silence expression in a specific tissue.

modules.title: Biotechnology & Genetic Engineering
modules.objectives: Explain the principles and steps of recombinant DNA technology; Describe the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and its applications; Explain how CRISPR-Cas9 enables precise genome editing; Discuss the applications and ethical implications of genetic engineering in medicine, agriculture, and forensics.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 17: Biotechnology and Genomics
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — DNA Cloning and Recombinant DNA
modules.assignment: Design a complete plan to produce insulin using recombinant DNA technology: describe restriction enzyme selection, vector choice, transformation method, selection of successful transformants, and purification strategy; then write a 200-word ethical analysis of one CRISPR application in human medicine.

modules.title: Evolution: Evidence & Mechanisms
modules.objectives: Summarize the multiple lines of evidence for evolution including fossil record, biogeography, comparative anatomy, and molecular biology; Describe the mechanisms of evolution including natural selection, mutation, drift, and gene flow; Explain the concept of evolutionary fitness; Distinguish between macroevolution and microevolution.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 18: Evolution and the Origin of Species
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Evidence for Evolution
modules.assignment: Compare and contrast four lines of evidence for evolution (fossil record, homologous structures, molecular evidence, and biogeography), providing a specific example for each; then explain why no single line of evidence is sufficient on its own.

modules.title: Population Genetics & Hardy-Weinberg
modules.titleZh: 种群遗传学与哈迪-温伯格定律
modules.objectives: State and apply the Hardy-Weinberg principle to calculate allele and genotype frequencies; Identify which conditions violate Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium; Use chi-square tests to evaluate whether a population is in equilibrium; Explain how natural selection changes allele frequencies over time.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 19: The Evolution of Populations
modules.assignment: Given a population of 500 individuals where 180 are homozygous recessive (aa), calculate allele frequencies (p and q), expected genotype frequencies, and expected numbers of each genotype; then perform a chi-square test with provided observed values and determine if the population is in equilibrium.

modules.title: Speciation & Macroevolution
modules.objectives: Distinguish between allopatric and sympatric speciation; Describe pre- and postzygotic reproductive isolating mechanisms; Explain the concepts of punctuated equilibrium and phyletic gradualism; Describe macroevolutionary patterns including adaptive radiation and mass extinctions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 18: Evolution and the Origin of Species
modules.assignment: Compare allopatric and sympatric speciation using a specific real example of each (e.g., Galapagos finches and apple maggot fly); for each example, identify the isolating mechanism(s) and explain the sequence of events leading to species divergence.

modules.title: Animal Physiology: Nervous System
modules.objectives: Describe the structure of a neuron and explain how action potentials are generated and propagated; Explain how synaptic transmission works at the chemical synapse; Describe the organization of the human nervous system (CNS and PNS); Compare the somatic and autonomic divisions of the PNS.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 35: The Nervous System
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — The Action Potential
modules.assignment: Trace the events of a reflex arc from stimulus detection to motor response, naming every structure involved; then compare and contrast excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials and explain how temporal and spatial summation determine whether a neuron will fire.

modules.title: Animal Physiology: Endocrine & Immune Systems
modules.titleZh: 动物生理学：内分泌与免疫系统
modules.objectives: Compare how the nervous and endocrine systems coordinate physiological responses; Describe the structure and mechanism of action of protein and steroid hormones; Distinguish between innate and adaptive immunity; Explain how B cells and T cells produce a specific immune response and immunological memory.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 37: The Endocrine System
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Endocrine and Immune System Practice
modules.assignment: Compare and contrast the mechanisms of action of a steroid hormone (e.g., cortisol) and a peptide hormone (e.g., insulin), including receptor location, signal transduction pathway, and speed of response; then explain how a COVID-19 vaccine produces immunological memory using the clonal selection model.

modules.title: Plant Biology & Adaptations
modules.objectives: Describe the alternation of generations life cycle in angiosperms; Explain the process of double fertilization and seed development; Describe plant responses to light (phototropism, photoperiodism) and gravity (gravitropism); Discuss adaptations of plants to arid environments (CAM and C4 photosynthesis).
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Bozeman Science — Plant Hormones and Tropisms
modules.assignment: Compare C3, C4, and CAM photosynthetic pathways, explaining the specific anatomical and biochemical adaptations each represents; state the environmental conditions in which each is advantageous and provide one example plant species for each pathway.

modules.title: Ecology: Energy Flow & Nutrient Cycles
modules.titleZh: 生态学：能量流动与物质循环
modules.objectives: Trace energy flow through ecosystems using trophic levels and ecological pyramids; Calculate net primary productivity (NPP) and gross primary productivity (GPP); Describe the carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus biogeochemical cycles; Analyze how human activities disrupt nutrient cycles (eutrophication, climate change).
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Food Chains and Energy Flow
modules.assignment: Using data from a given terrestrial ecosystem (NPP, organism biomass at each trophic level), construct a pyramid of energy and a pyramid of biomass; explain any inversions observed and describe how nitrogen inputs from agricultural runoff cause eutrophication in an adjacent lake.

modules.title: Conservation Biology
modules.objectives: Define biodiversity at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels; Identify the major threats to biodiversity (habitat destruction, invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, climate change); Explain the species-area relationship and its application to reserve design; Evaluate conservation strategies including protected areas, corridors, and ex situ conservation.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Biology 2e — Chapter 47: Conservation Biology and Biodiversity
modules.assignment: Using the IUCN Red List criteria, classify a given hypothetical species as Least Concern, Vulnerable, Endangered, or Critically Endangered based on provided population trend data; then propose a science-based conservation plan including at least three strategies and justify each with ecological principles.

quizQuestions.question: The endosymbiotic theory is supported by which observation about mitochondria?
options: Mitochondria have their own circular DNA and ribosomes similar to bacteria
Mitochondria are surrounded by a single membrane
quizQuestions.answer: Mitochondria have their own circular DNA and ribosomes similar to bacteria
quizQuestions.explanation: Mitochondria contain circular DNA and 70S ribosomes resembling those of alpha-proteobacteria, strongly supporting an endosymbiotic origin.

quizQuestions.question: Which cytoskeletal element is primarily responsible for forming the mitotic spindle?
quizQuestions.answer: Microtubules
quizQuestions.explanation: Microtubules form the spindle fibers that attach to kinetochores and separate chromosomes during mitosis.

quizQuestions.question: Vesicular transport from the rough ER to the Golgi apparatus is mediated by:
quizQuestions.answer: COPII-coated vesicles
quizQuestions.explanation: COPII-coated vesicles bud from the ER and fuse with the cis face of the Golgi apparatus as part of the secretory pathway.

quizQuestions.question: Which feature of prokaryotes is absent from eukaryotic cells?
quizQuestions.answer: 70S ribosomes
quizQuestions.explanation: Prokaryotes have 70S ribosomes (50S + 30S subunits); eukaryotes have 80S ribosomes (60S + 40S subunits).

quizQuestions.question: The primary function of the smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER) is:
quizQuestions.answer: Lipid synthesis and detoxification
quizQuestions.explanation: The SER lacks ribosomes and is specialized for lipid and steroid synthesis, carbohydrate metabolism, and drug detoxification.

quizQuestions.question: In enzyme kinetics, Km is defined as the substrate concentration at which:
options: Reaction velocity equals half of Vmax
quizQuestions.answer: Reaction velocity equals half of Vmax
quizQuestions.explanation: Km (Michaelis constant) is the substrate concentration at which the reaction proceeds at half the maximum velocity, reflecting enzyme-substrate affinity.

quizQuestions.question: A competitive inhibitor increases the apparent Km but does not change Vmax because:
options: It can be outcompeted by adding more substrate
It binds to the allosteric site irreversibly
It changes the enzyme's tertiary structure
quizQuestions.answer: It can be outcompeted by adding more substrate
quizQuestions.explanation: Competitive inhibitors bind reversibly to the active site; excess substrate displaces the inhibitor, restoring Vmax while effectively increasing the required substrate concentration (higher apparent Km).

quizQuestions.question: Which bond forms between amino acids during protein synthesis?
quizQuestions.answer: Peptide bond
quizQuestions.explanation: A peptide bond is a covalent bond formed between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another during translation.

quizQuestions.question: Allosteric regulation of an enzyme occurs when:
options: A substrate binds to the active site
A molecule binds to a site other than the active site and changes enzyme conformation
The enzyme is denatured by heat
A covalent modification permanently inactivates the enzyme
quizQuestions.answer: A molecule binds to a site other than the active site and changes enzyme conformation
quizQuestions.explanation: Allosteric effectors bind regulatory (allosteric) sites, inducing conformational changes that increase or decrease enzyme activity.

quizQuestions.question: Which level of protein structure involves interactions between multiple polypeptide subunits?
quizQuestions.answer: Quaternary
quizQuestions.explanation: Quaternary structure describes the arrangement of two or more polypeptide chains (subunits) into a single functional protein complex.

quizQuestions.question: Thomas Hunt Morgan's experiments with Drosophila established that genes are:
options: Equally distributed between sexes in all traits
quizQuestions.answer: Located on chromosomes
quizQuestions.explanation: Morgan's discovery of sex-linked inheritance in white-eyed flies linked specific genes to specific chromosomes, establishing the chromosomal theory.

quizQuestions.question: The map distance between two genes is expressed in centimorgans (cM) where 1 cM equals:
quizQuestions.answer: 1% recombination frequency
quizQuestions.explanation: One centimorgan equals 1% recombination frequency between two loci, derived from observed crossover percentages in testcrosses.

quizQuestions.question: Down syndrome results from which type of chromosomal abnormality?
quizQuestions.answer: Trisomy 21 (nondisjunction)
quizQuestions.explanation: Down syndrome is caused by nondisjunction during meiosis, resulting in three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21).

quizQuestions.question: Genes that are 50 cM apart on the same chromosome will be inherited:
options: Completely independently, as if on separate chromosomes
quizQuestions.answer: Completely independently, as if on separate chromosomes
quizQuestions.explanation: At 50 cM apart, recombination is so frequent that genes on the same chromosome assort independently, violating linkage expectations.

quizQuestions.question: In mammals, sex is determined by:
quizQuestions.answer: The XX/XY chromosomal system
quizQuestions.explanation: In mammals, females are XX and males are XY; the presence of a Y chromosome (specifically the SRY gene) triggers male development.

quizQuestions.question: Okazaki fragments are synthesized on the lagging strand because:
options: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction
Helicase unwinds DNA in only one direction
Primase cannot access the leading strand
The lagging strand lacks a template
quizQuestions.answer: DNA polymerase can only add nucleotides in the 5' to 3' direction
quizQuestions.explanation: Since polymerase adds to the 3' end, the lagging strand (running 3' to 5' toward the fork) must be synthesized in short segments (Okazaki fragments) away from the fork.

quizQuestions.question: Nucleotide excision repair (NER) corrects which type of DNA damage?
options: Bulky helix-distorting lesions such as thymine dimers
quizQuestions.answer: Bulky helix-distorting lesions such as thymine dimers
quizQuestions.explanation: NER recognizes helix distortions caused by UV-induced thymine dimers or other bulky adducts, excising and replacing a short oligonucleotide containing the damage.

quizQuestions.question: Which enzyme synthesizes the RNA primers required for DNA replication?
quizQuestions.answer: Primase
quizQuestions.explanation: Primase is an RNA polymerase that synthesizes a short RNA primer to provide a 3'-OH group for DNA polymerase to begin synthesis.

quizQuestions.question: BRCA1 and BRCA2 gene mutations increase breast cancer risk because these genes normally:
options: Repair double-strand DNA breaks via homologous recombination
quizQuestions.answer: Repair double-strand DNA breaks via homologous recombination
quizQuestions.explanation: BRCA1/2 are involved in homologous recombination repair of double-strand breaks; their loss leads to genomic instability and cancer.

quizQuestions.question: The 3' to 5' exonuclease activity of DNA polymerase provides:
quizQuestions.answer: Proofreading to remove misincorporated nucleotides
quizQuestions.explanation: The exonuclease activity allows DNA polymerase to remove an incorrectly incorporated nucleotide and replace it with the correct one, drastically reducing the error rate.

quizQuestions.question: Post-translational modification by phosphorylation typically:
options: Alters protein activity, localization, or interaction with other molecules
Prevents protein from leaving the ribosome
Adds a signal sequence for secretion
quizQuestions.answer: Alters protein activity, localization, or interaction with other molecules
quizQuestions.explanation: Phosphorylation adds a charged phosphate group to serine, threonine, or tyrosine, commonly switching proteins between active and inactive states.

quizQuestions.question: DNA methylation at gene promoter CpG islands is generally associated with:
quizQuestions.answer: Gene silencing
quizQuestions.explanation: Methylation of cytosines in CpG islands typically recruits repressor proteins and promotes chromatin compaction, silencing gene expression.

quizQuestions.question: Signal sequences on newly synthesized proteins direct them to:
options: The rough ER for processing and secretion
quizQuestions.answer: The rough ER for processing and secretion
quizQuestions.explanation: An N-terminal signal sequence targets a ribosome-nascent chain complex to the rough ER, where the protein is inserted for folding and eventual secretion.

quizQuestions.question: The proteome of a cell differs from its genome because:
options: The proteome contains only essential proteins
Proteins change with conditions while the genome is constant
The proteome has fewer molecules than the genome
quizQuestions.answer: Proteins change with conditions while the genome is constant
quizQuestions.explanation: A cell's proteome (the full set of proteins) varies with developmental stage, environment, and signals, even though the genome is essentially fixed.

quizQuestions.question: Which eukaryotic post-transcriptional modification increases mRNA stability and facilitates export?
quizQuestions.answer: Addition of a poly-A tail
quizQuestions.explanation: A poly-A tail of ~200 adenine nucleotides is added to the 3' end of eukaryotic mRNA, protecting it from degradation and aiding nuclear export and translation.

quizQuestions.question: Restriction enzymes are used in recombinant DNA technology because they:
options: Cut DNA at specific sequences, generating compatible ends
quizQuestions.answer: Cut DNA at specific sequences, generating compatible ends
quizQuestions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases cleave DNA at palindromic sequences, often generating cohesive (sticky) ends that facilitate joining with compatible vector DNA.

quizQuestions.question: During PCR, the purpose of the denaturation step is to:
options: Separate the double-stranded DNA template into single strands
Introduce mutations into the target sequence
quizQuestions.answer: Separate the double-stranded DNA template into single strands
quizQuestions.explanation: High temperature (~95°C) during denaturation breaks hydrogen bonds between base pairs, separating strands for primer annealing.

quizQuestions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 achieves genomic editing by:
options: Inserting random sequences into the genome
Using guide RNA to direct Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA sequence for cutting
Methylating target genes to silence them
quizQuestions.answer: Using guide RNA to direct Cas9 nuclease to a specific DNA sequence for cutting
quizQuestions.explanation: A single guide RNA (sgRNA) complementary to the target sequence directs Cas9 to cut both strands of DNA at that location.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a transgenic organism?
options: A plant produced by selective breeding
A bacterium containing a human insulin gene
A clone produced by somatic cell nuclear transfer
A species with a naturally occurring mutation
quizQuestions.answer: A bacterium containing a human insulin gene
quizQuestions.explanation: Transgenic organisms contain foreign DNA (from another species) introduced by genetic engineering, such as E. coli engineered to produce human insulin.

quizQuestions.question: Gel electrophoresis separates DNA fragments primarily based on:
quizQuestions.answer: Fragment size
quizQuestions.explanation: In an agarose gel with electric current, smaller DNA fragments migrate faster and further than larger fragments, separating them by size.

quizQuestions.question: Vestigial structures serve as evidence for evolution because they:
options: Are fully functional in all species
Represent structures inherited from ancestors in which they were functional
Are found only in extinct species
Disprove the need for natural selection
quizQuestions.answer: Represent structures inherited from ancestors in which they were functional
quizQuestions.explanation: Vestigial structures (e.g., human coccyx, whale pelvis) are remnants of structures that were functional in ancestral species, indicating common descent.

quizQuestions.question: Biogeography supports evolution because:
options: Identical species are found on all continents
Species distributions correlate with continental drift and common ancestry
All organisms have the same DNA sequence
Fossils are only found near the equator
quizQuestions.answer: Species distributions correlate with continental drift and common ancestry
quizQuestions.explanation: The geographic distribution of species—especially on islands and separated landmasses—correlates with evolutionary history and plate tectonics.

quizQuestions.question: Molecular evidence for evolution includes the observation that:
options: All organisms use different genetic codes
Cytochrome c protein sequences are more similar in closely related species
Proteins have no conserved sequences across species
Mutation rates are identical in all organisms
quizQuestions.answer: Cytochrome c protein sequences are more similar in closely related species
quizQuestions.explanation: Conserved molecular sequences like cytochrome c show fewer differences between more closely related species, reflecting shared ancestry.

quizQuestions.question: Macroevolution refers to:
options: Changes in allele frequency within a population over one generation
Evolution occurring above the species level, such as the origin of new phyla
The process of natural selection on individual organisms
Random genetic drift in small populations
quizQuestions.answer: Evolution occurring above the species level, such as the origin of new phyla
quizQuestions.explanation: Macroevolution encompasses large-scale evolutionary changes including the origins of new higher taxonomic groups, adaptive radiations, and mass extinctions.

quizQuestions.question: Analogous structures in distantly related organisms provide evidence for:
quizQuestions.answer: Convergent evolution
quizQuestions.explanation: Analogous structures (similar function, different evolutionary origin) result from convergent evolution where unrelated species independently evolve similar adaptations.

quizQuestions.question: If the frequency of the recessive allele (q) in a population is 0.3, what is the frequency of the dominant allele (p)?
quizQuestions.explanation: In a two-allele system, p + q = 1, so p = 1 − 0.3 = 0.7.

quizQuestions.question: Under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, the expected frequency of heterozygotes (Aa) is:
quizQuestions.answer: 2pq
quizQuestions.explanation: The Hardy-Weinberg equation p² + 2pq + q² = 1 gives the heterozygote frequency as 2pq.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following would cause a population to deviate from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
quizQuestions.answer: Assortative mating
quizQuestions.explanation: Assortative mating (non-random mating by phenotype) violates the random mating requirement of Hardy-Weinberg, changing genotype frequencies.

quizQuestions.question: If the observed and expected genotype frequencies are very different (large chi-square value), you would:
options: Conclude that allele frequencies are equal
quizQuestions.answer: Reject Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
quizQuestions.explanation: A large chi-square value indicates that observed frequencies differ significantly from those expected under HWE, suggesting evolutionary forces are acting.

quizQuestions.question: Directional selection on a trait will cause the allele frequency distribution to:
quizQuestions.answer: Shift toward the favored extreme
quizQuestions.explanation: Directional selection consistently favors one extreme phenotype, gradually increasing the frequency of the advantageous alleles and shifting the population mean.

quizQuestions.question: Allopatric speciation requires:
quizQuestions.answer: Geographic isolation preventing gene flow
quizQuestions.explanation: Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are physically separated (e.g., by mountains or ocean), preventing interbreeding and allowing independent divergence.

quizQuestions.question: Polyploidy as a mechanism of speciation is most common in:
quizQuestions.answer: Plants
quizQuestions.explanation: Polyploidy (having multiple chromosome sets) is a common speciation mechanism in plants and rarely occurs in animals.

quizQuestions.question: Hybrid vigor (heterosis) is the observation that:
options: Hybrids between different strains often outperform either parent
Hybrids revert to one parental species
quizQuestions.answer: Hybrids between different strains often outperform either parent
quizQuestions.explanation: Heterosis is the enhanced performance of hybrid offspring, often attributed to increased heterozygosity masking deleterious recessive alleles.

quizQuestions.question: Punctuated equilibrium proposes that speciation:
options: Occurs gradually over millions of years
Occurs rapidly in geological time followed by long periods of stasis
Is driven entirely by genetic drift
quizQuestions.answer: Occurs rapidly in geological time followed by long periods of stasis
quizQuestions.explanation: Punctuated equilibrium, proposed by Gould and Eldredge, suggests species change little for long periods but undergo rapid change during speciation events.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a postzygotic reproductive isolating mechanism?
quizQuestions.answer: Hybrid inviability
quizQuestions.explanation: Hybrid inviability occurs when hybrids are formed but fail to survive to reproductive age, a postzygotic barrier that occurs after fertilization.

quizQuestions.question: In a resting neuron, the membrane potential is approximately:
quizQuestions.answer: −70 mV
quizQuestions.explanation: The resting membrane potential of a typical mammalian neuron is approximately −70 mV, maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase and selective ion channels.

quizQuestions.question: The refractory period after an action potential ensures:
options: Unidirectional propagation of the action potential
quizQuestions.answer: Unidirectional propagation of the action potential
quizQuestions.explanation: During the refractory period, Na⁺ channels are inactivated, preventing the action potential from traveling backward.

quizQuestions.question: Saltatory conduction occurs in myelinated neurons because the action potential:
options: Is slower due to myelin resistance
Jumps between nodes of Ranvier, increasing speed
Travels along the entire axon membrane
quizQuestions.answer: Jumps between nodes of Ranvier, increasing speed
quizQuestions.explanation: Myelin insulates the axon between nodes; depolarization jumps from one node to the next (saltatory), greatly increasing conduction velocity.

quizQuestions.question: Which neurotransmitter is released at the neuromuscular junction?
quizQuestions.answer: Acetylcholine
quizQuestions.explanation: Acetylcholine (ACh) is released by motor neurons at the neuromuscular junction, binding nicotinic receptors on muscle cells to trigger contraction.

quizQuestions.question: GABA is classified as an inhibitory neurotransmitter because it:
options: Opens Na⁺ channels causing an EPSP
Increases neurotransmitter release at the presynaptic terminal
quizQuestions.answer: Opens Cl⁻ channels causing hyperpolarization (IPSP)
quizQuestions.explanation: GABA binds GABA-A receptors, opening Cl⁻ channels that hyperpolarize the postsynaptic membrane, making it less likely to fire an action potential.

quizQuestions.question: Steroid hormones differ from protein hormones in that steroid hormones:
options: Bind to intracellular receptors and directly regulate transcription
Act through second messengers like cAMP
quizQuestions.answer: Bind to intracellular receptors and directly regulate transcription
quizQuestions.explanation: Lipid-soluble steroid hormones diffuse across the plasma membrane and bind nuclear receptors, which act as transcription factors.

quizQuestions.question: The hypothalamus regulates the pituitary gland through:
options: Releasing and inhibiting hormones secreted into the portal blood supply
Paracrine signaling at the pituitary surface
quizQuestions.answer: Releasing and inhibiting hormones secreted into the portal blood supply
quizQuestions.explanation: Hypothalamic hormones travel via the hypophyseal portal system to the anterior pituitary, stimulating or inhibiting the release of pituitary hormones.

quizQuestions.question: B lymphocytes contribute to adaptive immunity by:
options: Differentiating into plasma cells that secrete antibodies
quizQuestions.answer: Differentiating into plasma cells that secrete antibodies
quizQuestions.explanation: When activated by antigen and T helper cells, B cells differentiate into plasma cells that produce and secrete antigen-specific antibodies.

quizQuestions.question: Memory cells in the adaptive immune response allow:
options: A faster and stronger response upon re-exposure to the same antigen
quizQuestions.answer: A faster and stronger response upon re-exposure to the same antigen
quizQuestions.explanation: Memory B and T cells persist long-term after the primary immune response and mount a rapid, amplified secondary response to the same antigen.

quizQuestions.question: Autoimmune diseases result from the immune system:
quizQuestions.answer: Attacking the body's own tissues
quizQuestions.explanation: Autoimmune diseases occur when immune tolerance breaks down and the immune system mistakenly targets self-antigens, as in type 1 diabetes and rheumatoid arthritis.

quizQuestions.question: Double fertilization in angiosperms produces:
options: One embryo (2n) and one endosperm nucleus (3n)
A diploid embryo and a haploid seed coat
quizQuestions.answer: One embryo (2n) and one endosperm nucleus (3n)
quizQuestions.explanation: One sperm fuses with the egg to form the diploid (2n) embryo; the second sperm fuses with two polar nuclei to form the triploid (3n) endosperm.

quizQuestions.question: C4 plants avoid photorespiration by:
options: Pre-fixing CO₂ into a 4-carbon compound in mesophyll cells before passing it to bundle sheath cells
Using CAM to fix CO₂ only when stomata are open at night
quizQuestions.answer: Pre-fixing CO₂ into a 4-carbon compound in mesophyll cells before passing it to bundle sheath cells
quizQuestions.explanation: C4 plants spatially separate CO₂ fixation: PEP carboxylase in mesophyll concentrates CO₂ for RuBisCO in bundle sheath cells, minimizing photorespiration.

quizQuestions.question: Phototropism in plants is primarily regulated by:
quizQuestions.answer: Auxin (IAA)
quizQuestions.explanation: Auxin redistributes to the shaded side of a shoot, promoting cell elongation there and causing the plant to bend toward light.

quizQuestions.question: Long-day plants flower when:
options: Total daylight exceeds a critical maximum
Darkness is shorter than a critical night length
Temperature drops below a critical threshold
quizQuestions.answer: Darkness is shorter than a critical night length
quizQuestions.explanation: Photoperiodism is actually controlled by the length of the dark period; 'long-day' plants require nights shorter than a critical length to flower.

quizQuestions.question: CAM photosynthesis is an adaptation to arid environments because CAM plants:
options: Open stomata during the day and close at night
Close stomata during the day and fix CO₂ at night into organic acids
Absorb water directly through their leaves
quizQuestions.answer: Close stomata during the day and fix CO₂ at night into organic acids
quizQuestions.explanation: CAM plants open stomata at night to fix CO₂ (stored as malate), then close stomata during the day to minimize water loss while using stored CO₂ in the Calvin cycle.

quizQuestions.question: Net primary productivity (NPP) is defined as:
options: Gross primary productivity minus respiration losses by producers
Total biomass at the producer level
quizQuestions.answer: Gross primary productivity minus respiration losses by producers
quizQuestions.explanation: NPP = GPP − Ra (autotroph respiration); it represents the energy actually available to consumers.

quizQuestions.question: Eutrophication of lakes occurs when:
options: Excess nutrients (N, P) stimulate algal blooms that deplete oxygen when they decompose
Predatory fish are removed from the lake
Acid rain lowers the lake pH
quizQuestions.answer: Excess nutrients (N, P) stimulate algal blooms that deplete oxygen when they decompose
quizQuestions.explanation: Nutrient loading from agricultural runoff causes algal blooms; decomposition of dead algae consumes O₂, creating hypoxic dead zones.

quizQuestions.question: Which process in the phosphorus cycle is absent (unlike in the carbon and nitrogen cycles)?
quizQuestions.answer: Atmospheric gas phase
quizQuestions.explanation: Unlike carbon and nitrogen, phosphorus has no significant gaseous phase; it cycles primarily through rock weathering, soil, organisms, and aquatic sediments.

quizQuestions.question: An inverted pyramid of biomass can occur in aquatic ecosystems because:
options: Fish have more biomass than algae
Phytoplankton have a very high turnover rate relative to their standing biomass
quizQuestions.answer: Phytoplankton have a very high turnover rate relative to their standing biomass
quizQuestions.explanation: Phytoplankton reproduce very rapidly; although their standing stock is low, their productivity is high, supporting much greater zooplankton biomass.

quizQuestions.question: The greenhouse effect is enhanced by human activities primarily through:
options: Elevated atmospheric CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion trapping infrared radiation
Increased precipitation washing CO₂ from the air
quizQuestions.answer: Elevated atmospheric CO₂ from fossil fuel combustion trapping infrared radiation
quizQuestions.explanation: CO₂ and other greenhouse gases trap outgoing infrared radiation, causing the atmosphere to warm—an effect intensified by fossil fuel burning.

quizQuestions.question: The species-area relationship states that:
options: Larger areas always have fewer species
The number of species increases with increasing area, typically following a power law
Species richness is unrelated to habitat area
quizQuestions.answer: The number of species increases with increasing area, typically following a power law
quizQuestions.explanation: The species-area relationship (S = cA^z) shows that larger habitat patches support more species, critical for reserve design.

quizQuestions.question: The primary driver of biodiversity loss worldwide is:
quizQuestions.answer: Habitat destruction and fragmentation
quizQuestions.explanation: Habitat loss and fragmentation remain the leading cause of biodiversity decline globally, followed by invasive species, overexploitation, pollution, and climate change.

quizQuestions.question: Ex situ conservation refers to:
options: Protecting species in their natural habitat
Conserving species outside their natural habitat (zoos, seed banks)
quizQuestions.answer: Conserving species outside their natural habitat (zoos, seed banks)
quizQuestions.explanation: Ex situ conservation (e.g., zoos, botanical gardens, cryobanks) preserves species or genetic material away from the natural environment.

quizQuestions.question: Wildlife corridors are designed to:
options: Connect fragmented habitat patches to allow gene flow and migration
quizQuestions.answer: Connect fragmented habitat patches to allow gene flow and migration
quizQuestions.explanation: Corridors link isolated habitat fragments, enabling animals to move between patches, increasing effective population size and genetic diversity.

quizQuestions.question: Biodiversity hotspots are defined as regions that:
options: Have the highest rainfall and temperature globally
Have exceptional species richness and endemism with significant habitat loss
Are fully protected from human disturbance
quizQuestions.answer: Have exceptional species richness and endemism with significant habitat loss
quizQuestions.explanation: Biodiversity hotspots (e.g., Sundaland, Mediterranean Basin) are characterized by high endemic species richness and having lost at least 70% of original habitat.

questions.question: The endosymbiotic theory is best supported by the fact that mitochondria:
options: Are enclosed by only one membrane
Divide by binary fission and have circular DNA similar to bacteria
Are found in all prokaryotic cells
Use the same ribosomes as the cytoplasm
questions.answer: Divide by binary fission and have circular DNA similar to bacteria
questions.explanation: Mitochondria divide like bacteria, have their own circular genome, and have 70S ribosomes, all consistent with a bacterial endosymbiont origin.

questions.question: In enzyme kinetics, a noncompetitive inhibitor:
options: Increases the apparent Km without changing Vmax
Competes with substrate at the active site
Is overcome by adding excess substrate
questions.answer: Decreases Vmax without changing Km
questions.explanation: Noncompetitive inhibitors bind a site other than the active site; since they don't block substrate binding, Km is unchanged, but Vmax decreases.

questions.question: A testcross between a double heterozygote (AaBb) and a homozygous recessive (aabb) gives 48% AB, 48% ab, 2% Ab, 2% aB offspring. The genes are:
options: Linked with about 4 cM between them
questions.answer: Linked with about 4 cM between them
questions.explanation: Recombinant classes (Ab + aB = 4%) indicate the genes are linked with a map distance of approximately 4 centimorgans.

questions.question: Which of the following enzymes removes RNA primers and fills the gap with DNA during replication?
questions.answer: DNA polymerase I
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase I has 5' to 3' exonuclease activity that removes RNA primers while simultaneously replacing them with DNA.

questions.question: Histone acetylation generally leads to:
options: Chromatin relaxation and increased gene expression
questions.answer: Chromatin relaxation and increased gene expression
questions.explanation: Acetylation of histone tails neutralizes positive charges, loosening histone-DNA interactions and making genes more accessible for transcription.

questions.question: What distinguishes CRISPR-Cas9 from older gene-editing tools like zinc finger nucleases?
options: CRISPR can only silence genes, not cut DNA
CRISPR uses a programmable RNA guide rather than engineered proteins for targeting
CRISPR is limited to plant cells
questions.answer: CRISPR uses a programmable RNA guide rather than engineered proteins for targeting
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9's programmability comes from a guide RNA that can be easily redesigned, making it far faster and cheaper than protein-based nucleases.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of the poly-A tail on eukaryotic mRNA?
options: It serves as the ribosome binding site
It provides the start codon for translation
It protects the mRNA from degradation and aids in nuclear export
It codes for a string of lysine residues
questions.answer: It protects the mRNA from degradation and aids in nuclear export
questions.explanation: The poly-A tail stabilizes mRNA by protecting the 3' end from exonuclease attack and facilitates export from the nucleus.

questions.question: In a Hardy-Weinberg population where q = 0.4, the expected frequency of homozygous dominant individuals (AA) is:
questions.explanation: p = 1 − 0.4 = 0.6; frequency of AA = p² = (0.6)² = 0.36.

questions.question: Homologous structures in different species suggest:
options: Identical ecological roles in different environments
questions.answer: Common ancestry with modifications
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share the same underlying anatomy derived from a common ancestor, even if they perform different functions (e.g., human arm, whale flipper, bat wing).

questions.question: What is the threshold potential at which an action potential is initiated?
questions.answer: −55 mV
questions.explanation: When the membrane potential reaches approximately −55 mV (threshold), voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open, triggering a full action potential.

questions.question: The function of Okazaki fragments is to:
options: Allow the lagging strand to be synthesized in short segments in the 5' to 3' direction
Provide primers for leading strand synthesis
questions.answer: Allow the lagging strand to be synthesized in short segments in the 5' to 3' direction
questions.explanation: DNA polymerase can only synthesize 5' to 3', so the lagging strand is made in short Okazaki fragments discontinuously, later joined by ligase.

questions.question: Which type of reproductive isolation prevents mating due to different habitat preferences within the same geographic area?
questions.answer: Ecological (habitat) isolation
questions.explanation: Ecological isolation is a prezygotic barrier where two species occupy different microhabitats in the same region and rarely encounter each other.

questions.question: Transformation in recombinant DNA technology refers to:
options: Introducing foreign DNA into a bacterial cell
questions.answer: Introducing foreign DNA into a bacterial cell
questions.explanation: Transformation is the uptake and expression of foreign DNA (e.g., a plasmid with a gene of interest) by a bacterial host cell.

questions.question: Feedback inhibition of a metabolic pathway occurs when:
options: A substrate activates the first enzyme of its own pathway
The end product inhibits an enzyme earlier in its own synthesis pathway
An enzyme is activated by a positive allosteric effector
Two pathways compete for the same substrate
questions.answer: The end product inhibits an enzyme earlier in its own synthesis pathway
questions.explanation: Feedback (end-product) inhibition is a regulatory mechanism where the accumulation of the end product allosterically inhibits an early enzyme, preventing overproduction.

questions.question: Which evidence would most directly demonstrate that a particular DNA sequence functions as a gene promoter?
options: Showing that deletion of the sequence eliminates transcription of the downstream gene
Showing the sequence is near the 3' end of the gene
questions.answer: Showing that deletion of the sequence eliminates transcription of the downstream gene
questions.explanation: Deleting a putative promoter and observing loss of transcription provides direct functional evidence that the sequence is required for gene expression.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the function of lysosomes?
options: Synthesize lipids for the plasma membrane
Digest cellular debris and foreign material using hydrolytic enzymes
questions.answer: Digest cellular debris and foreign material using hydrolytic enzymes
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain acid hydrolases that break down macromolecules from autophagy, endocytosis, and phagocytosis.

questions.question: Nucleotide excision repair (NER) is initiated when:
options: A mismatch in base pairing is detected after replication
Helix-distorting damage such as a thymine dimer is recognized
A depurination event removes a guanine base
questions.answer: Helix-distorting damage such as a thymine dimer is recognized
questions.explanation: NER repair proteins scan for and recognize helix distortions, then excise a short oligonucleotide containing the lesion for resynthesis.

questions.question: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing most commonly introduces mutations by:
options: Inserting a specific transgene without any cutting
Creating a double-strand break that is repaired imperfectly by NHEJ
Methylating the target gene to silence it
Replacing the target gene via homologous recombination in all cells
questions.answer: Creating a double-strand break that is repaired imperfectly by NHEJ
questions.explanation: Cas9 cuts both DNA strands; repair by nonhomologous end joining (NHEJ) frequently introduces insertions or deletions (indels) that disrupt gene function.

questions.question: The bottleneck effect most directly results in:
options: Increased genetic diversity due to founder effects
Reduced genetic diversity and increased inbreeding depression risk
Higher mutation rates in the surviving population
questions.answer: Reduced genetic diversity and increased inbreeding depression risk
questions.explanation: A bottleneck drastically reduces population size, randomly eliminating many alleles and reducing heterozygosity, often leading to inbreeding problems.

questions.question: In the nitrogen cycle, denitrifying bacteria convert:
questions.answer: NO₃⁻ into N₂
questions.explanation: Denitrifying bacteria (e.g., Pseudomonas) reduce nitrate (NO₃⁻) to nitrogen gas (N₂) under anaerobic conditions, completing the nitrogen cycle.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of steroid hormones?
options: They bind membrane receptors and activate second messenger cascades
They bind intracellular receptors and regulate gene transcription directly
They are water-soluble and transported freely in blood
They act only locally via paracrine signaling
questions.answer: They bind intracellular receptors and regulate gene transcription directly
questions.explanation: Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble; they diffuse into cells and bind cytoplasmic or nuclear receptors that act as transcription factors.

questions.question: The map distance in centimorgans between two linked genes is determined by:
options: The physical distance between them in base pairs only
The percent recombinant offspring in a testcross
The ratio of homozygous to heterozygous offspring
The number of introns between the two genes
questions.answer: The percent recombinant offspring in a testcross
questions.explanation: Recombination frequency (% recombinant offspring) from a testcross is used to calculate map distance: 1% recombination = 1 cM.

questions.question: An action potential is described as 'all-or-nothing' because:
options: It only occurs in all neurons simultaneously
Once threshold is reached, the full amplitude action potential always fires; stimuli below threshold produce nothing
The neuron fires continuously without stopping
All neurons have identical action potential amplitudes in the body
questions.answer: Once threshold is reached, the full amplitude action potential always fires; stimuli below threshold produce nothing
questions.explanation: Below threshold, no action potential is generated; at or above threshold, a full-amplitude action potential is always produced, regardless of stimulus strength.

questions.question: C4 photosynthesis reduces photorespiration by:
options: Concentrating CO₂ near RuBisCO in bundle sheath cells
Eliminating RuBisCO from the plant entirely
Opening stomata only at night to fix CO₂
questions.answer: Concentrating CO₂ near RuBisCO in bundle sheath cells
questions.explanation: C4 plants spatially separate carbon fixation, concentrating CO₂ so RuBisCO in bundle sheath cells operates in a high CO₂ environment, suppressing its oxygenase activity.

questions.question: The species-area relationship predicts that when a habitat is reduced to 10% of its original size, species richness will decline to approximately:
questions.answer: 50% of original
questions.explanation: Using the species-area relationship with a typical z-value of ~0.3, reducing area to 10% (10^0.3 ≈ 2) roughly halves species richness.

questions.question: Which of the following best explains why antibiotics that inhibit 70S ribosomes can selectively kill bacteria without harming human cells?
options: Human cells have 80S ribosomes that are not affected by these antibiotics
Human cells can repair ribosomal damage quickly
Bacteria have thicker cell walls that concentrate the antibiotic
questions.answer: Human cells have 80S ribosomes that are not affected by these antibiotics
questions.explanation: Structural differences between prokaryotic 70S and eukaryotic 80S ribosomes allow antibiotics like streptomycin to target bacteria selectively.

questions.question: Allopatric speciation and sympatric speciation differ primarily in:
options: Whether geographic isolation is required for divergence
questions.answer: Whether geographic isolation is required for divergence
questions.explanation: Allopatric speciation requires geographic isolation to prevent gene flow; sympatric speciation occurs within the same range, often via polyploidy or ecological specialization.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes double fertilization in flowering plants?
options: Two sperm fuse with two eggs to produce two embryos
One sperm fertilizes the egg (forming 2n embryo) and the other sperm fuses with two polar nuclei (forming 3n endosperm)
One sperm fertilizes the egg and the other sperm degenerates
Two sperm fuse with the central cell only
questions.answer: One sperm fertilizes the egg (forming 2n embryo) and the other sperm fuses with two polar nuclei (forming 3n endosperm)
questions.explanation: Double fertilization is unique to angiosperms; it produces a diploid embryo and a triploid endosperm (nutritive tissue) simultaneously.

questions.question: Eutrophication in aquatic ecosystems ultimately reduces biodiversity because:
options: Oxygen depletion during algal bloom decomposition creates hypoxic dead zones
It increases light penetration to the benthos
questions.answer: Oxygen depletion during algal bloom decomposition creates hypoxic dead zones
questions.explanation: Decomposer bacteria consume oxygen breaking down dead algae, creating hypoxia that kills fish and other aerobic organisms.

questions.question: Wildlife corridors are most important for which ecological process?
options: Maintaining gene flow between fragmented populations
questions.answer: Maintaining gene flow between fragmented populations
questions.explanation: Corridors allow animals to move between patches, sustaining gene flow that maintains genetic diversity and reduces inbreeding in isolated populations.

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of K-selected species?
options: Small body size and rapid development
Long lifespan with extensive parental care and few offspring
Explosive population growth in disturbed habitats
questions.answer: Long lifespan with extensive parental care and few offspring
questions.explanation: K-selected species invest heavily in fewer offspring, have long lifespans, and thrive in stable environments near carrying capacity.

questions.question: The primary role of decomposers in biogeochemical cycles is to:
options: Break down organic matter and return inorganic nutrients to the environment
Transfer energy from producers to primary consumers
questions.answer: Break down organic matter and return inorganic nutrients to the environment
questions.explanation: Decomposers mineralize dead organic material, releasing nutrients like carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus back into the abiotic environment.

questions.question: Which of the following types of natural selection maintains two or more phenotypic forms in a population at stable frequencies?
questions.answer: Balancing selection (heterozygote advantage)
questions.explanation: Balancing selection (e.g., heterozygote advantage as in sickle-cell trait) maintains multiple alleles at stable intermediate frequencies in a population.

questions.question: In the adaptive immune response, cytotoxic T cells (CD8⁺) destroy infected cells by recognizing:
options: Antigen fragments presented on MHC class I molecules of infected cells
Antigens presented on MHC class II molecules of APCs
questions.answer: Antigen fragments presented on MHC class I molecules of infected cells
questions.explanation: Cytotoxic T cells monitor MHC class I molecules on all nucleated cells; infected cells display foreign peptides on MHC I, triggering CTL-mediated killing.

questions.question: A biodiversity hotspot is defined as an area that:
options: Has the highest rainfall on Earth
Contains at least 1,500 endemic plant species AND has lost at least 70% of its original habitat
Is fully protected from human activity
Has more introduced species than native species
questions.answer: Contains at least 1,500 endemic plant species AND has lost at least 70% of its original habitat
questions.explanation: Conservation International defines biodiversity hotspots by two criteria: exceptional endemism (≥1,500 endemic plant species) and severe habitat loss (≥70% original vegetation lost).
